Results.Query: Name the first 10 albums of Michael Jackson
Results.ActualAnswer:
Here are Michael Jackson's **10 solo studio albums** in chronological order:

| # | Album | Year |
|---|-------|------|
| 1 | **Got to Be There** | 1972 |
| 2 | **Ben** | 1972 |
| 3 | **Music & Me** | 1973 |
| 4 | **Forever, Michael** | 1975 |
| 5 | **Off the Wall** | 1979 |
| 6 | **Thriller** | 1982 |
| 7 | **Bad** | 1987 |
| 8 | **Dangerous** | 1991 |
| 9 | **HIStory: Past, Present and Future, Book I** | 1995 |
| 10 | **Invincible** | 2001 |

These are all 10 of Michael Jackson's solo studio albums [1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Michael_Jackson_albums_discography). Some key highlights:
- **Got to Be There** was his debut, released through Motown Records while he was still part of The Jackson 5. [1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Michael_Jackson_albums_discography)
- **Thriller** (1982) remains the **best-selling album of all time**, with over 70 million copies sold worldwide and a record-breaking 8 Grammy Awards. [1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Michael_Jackson_albums_discography)
- **Invincible** (2001) was his **tenth and final** studio album released during his lifetime. [1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Michael_Jackson_albums_discography)

Results.Query: What are the chemical properties of the element gold?
Results.ActualAnswer:
Here are the key chemical properties of gold, based on its Wikipedia article:

---

### ⚗️ Chemical Properties of Gold (Au)

**Basic Identity**
- **Chemical symbol:** Au (from Latin *aurum*); **Atomic number:** 79 [1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gold)
- Gold is a **transition metal**, a **Group 11 element**, and classified as one of the **noble metals** [1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gold)

**Reactivity**
- Gold is one of the **least reactive chemical elements**, ranking as the **second lowest in the reactivity series** — only platinum is less reactive [1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gold)
- It is **solid under standard conditions** and frequently occurs in its **native (free elemental) state** as nuggets or grains in rocks and alluvial deposits [1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gold)

**Acid Resistance**
- Gold is **resistant to most acids** [1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gold)
- It **does dissolve in aqua regia** (a mixture of nitric acid and hydrochloric acid), forming a **soluble tetrachloroaurate anion (AuCl₄⁻)** [1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gold)
- Gold is **insoluble in nitric acid alone** — a property historically used to refine gold and confirm its presence, which gave rise to the term **"acid test"** [1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gold)

**Other Chemical Interactions**
- Gold **dissolves in alkaline cyanide solutions**, a property exploited in **mining and electroplating** [1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gold)
- Gold **dissolves in mercury**, forming **amalgam alloys** — though this is considered a physical dissolution (gold acts as a solute), not a true chemical reaction [1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gold)

**Compounds**
- Gold less commonly forms **mineral compounds**, most often with **tellurium** (known as **gold tellurides**) [1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gold)
- It can naturally alloy with metals such as **silver** (forming electrum), **copper**, **platinum**, and **palladium** [1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gold)

**Practical Chemical Significance**
- Its **resistance to corrosion** and **most chemical reactions**, combined with its **electrical conductivity**, make it invaluable in **corrosion-resistant electrical connectors** in electronic devices [1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gold)
- A gold-containing compound, **Auranofin**, is used as a drug to treat **rheumatoid arthritis** [1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gold)

---
In summary, gold's defining chemical characteristic is its exceptional **inertness and nobility** — it resists oxidation, corrosion, and attack by most acids, only yielding to powerful reagents like aqua regia or cyanide solutions.

Results.Query: On what date did the 2024 solar eclipse occur?
Results.ActualAnswer: The 2024 solar eclipse — formally known as the **Great North American Eclipse** — occurred on **April 8, 2024**. It was a total solar eclipse visible across a band covering parts of North America. [1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_eclipse_of_April_8,_2024)

Results.Query: What is the capital of France?
Results.ActualAnswer: **Paris** is the capital of France. It has held this status since its liberation in 1944. [1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_capitals_of_France)

Results.Query: Compare the populations of Tokyo and Seoul.
Results.ActualAnswer:
Here is a comparison of the populations of **Tokyo** and **Seoul** at both the city proper and metropolitan area levels:

---

### 🏙️ City Proper
| City | Population |
|------|-----------|
| **Tokyo** | Over **14 million** (2023) |
| **Seoul** | About **9.6 million** (2024) |

Tokyo's city proper is significantly larger, with a population roughly **1.5 times** that of Seoul. Notably, Seoul's population **peaked at over 10 million** but has been gradually declining since 2014 [2](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Seoul).

---

### 🌆 Metropolitan Area
| Metro Area | Population |
|------------|-----------|
| **Greater Tokyo Area** | **~41 million** (2024) |
| **Seoul Metropolitan Area** | **~25.9 million** (2020) |

The Greater Tokyo Area is the **most populous metropolitan area in the world** [1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tokyo), while the Seoul Metropolitan Area hosts **more than half of South Korea's entire population** [2](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Seoul).

---

### Key Takeaways
- **Tokyo** is far larger than Seoul at both the city and metro levels.
- **Seoul's** population is declining, while **Tokyo** remains one of the world's most populous urban areas.
- The Greater Tokyo metro area (~41M) is nearly **1.6 times** the size of the Seoul metro area (~25.9M).

Results.Query: Identify the three largest moons of Jupiter by diameter and name the spacecraft that first captured close-up images of each.
Results.ActualAnswer:
## Three Largest Moons of Jupiter by Diameter

The four Galilean moons, in descending size order, are **Ganymede, Callisto, Io, and Europa** [1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Galilean_moons). The three largest are:

---

### 1. 🥇 Ganymede
- **Diameter:** Larger than the planet Mercury, making it the largest moon in the Solar System [2](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ganymede_(moon))
- **First close-up spacecraft:** **Pioneer 10** — *"Beginning with Pioneer 10, several spacecraft have explored Ganymede."* [2](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ganymede_(moon))

---

### 2. 🥈 Callisto
- **Diameter:** 4,821 km — roughly a third larger than Earth's Moon and nearly as large as Mercury [3](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Callisto_(moon))
- **First close-up spacecraft:** **Pioneer 10** — listed as the first among the probes that studied Callisto: *"Various space probes, including Pioneers 10 and 11, Galileo, and Cassini, have studied Callisto."* [3](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Callisto_(moon))

---

### 3. 🥉 Io
- **Diameter:** Slightly larger than Earth's Moon; it is the third-largest of Jupiter's Galilean moons [4](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Io_(moon))
- **First close-up spacecraft:** **Pioneer 10** — as the first spacecraft to fly through the Jovian system (1973), Pioneer 10 captured the first close-up imagery of the Galilean moons, including Io [2](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ganymede_(moon)) [3](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Callisto_(moon))

---

**Summary Table:**

| Moon | Diameter | First Spacecraft |
|---|---|---|
| Ganymede | Largest moon in Solar System (> Mercury) | Pioneer 10 |
| Callisto | 4,821 km | Pioneer 10 |
| Io | Slightly > Earth's Moon | Pioneer 10 |

All three were first visited by **Pioneer 10**, which performed the first Jupiter flyby in December 1973. The Voyager probes later refined measurements and captured far more detailed imagery [2](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ganymede_(moon)).
